skill.name: top-100-web-vulnerabilities-reference
skill.description: Reference guide for identifying, explaining, and prioritizing common web application vulnerabilities across major security categories.
skill.summary: Use an OWASP-aligned catalog to understand web vulnerability causes, impacts, and mitigations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives caused by a security reference document that names vulnerability classes, impacts, and mitigations. No executable code, shell invocation, credential access, network request, or prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md. The remaining concern is low dual-use risk because the skill discusses vulnerability verification techniques.

low_findings.title: Dual-use vulnerability testing guidance
low_findings.description: The skill includes high-level verification techniques such as payload testing, cross-origin form testing, SSRF callbacks, and XXE checks. This is legitimate security assessment guidance, but marketplace users should apply it only to authorized systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text clearly describes security testing approaches, but it does not provide exploit code or commands. The concern is dual-use context rather than malicious implementation.

low_findings.title: Static command and reconnaissance alerts are educational references
low_findings.description: Static alerts for command execution, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance are references to vulnerability impacts or defensive assessment concepts. They do not instruct the assistant to execute commands and do not contain runnable shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines define attack categories and mitigations in prose. No executable command, command interpolation, or user-controlled shell invocation appears there.

low_findings.title: Static weak-cryptography and Keychain alerts are taxonomy entries
low_findings.description: Static alerts for weak cryptography, deserialization, and Keychain access are caused by descriptive vulnerability catalog entries. The text recommends secure storage and modern transport controls rather than accessing secrets or implementing weak crypto.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain-language reference material in a table or vulnerability description. It does not call platform credential APIs or use cryptographic libraries.

low_findings.title: Static external command alert is a security header example
low_findings.description: The external command alert at the flagged location is a fenced example of HTTP security response headers. It is not Ruby code, shell code, or a command execution pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code fence contains only HTTP header names and values. There is no interpreter directive, process call, or executable syntax.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss issues when vulnerability categories are scattered across many sources. This skill provides a structured catalog of web risks, causes, impacts, and mitigations.
actual_capabilities: Explains common web vulnerability categories with root causes and impacts.
Maps vulnerabilities to OWASP Top 10 categories.
Summarizes mitigation strategies for injection, access control, API, SSRF, and client-side risks.
Provides quick reference tables for security reviews and remediation planning.
Highlights assessment challenges and manual verification approaches.
limitations: It is a reference guide, not an automated scanner.
It does not validate a specific application without user-provided context.
Mitigations must be adapted to the target technology stack.
The catalog can become outdated as new vulnerabilities emerge.

use_cases.title: Prepare an application security review
use_cases.description: Use the catalog to build a checklist before reviewing a web application or API.

use_cases.title: Explain vulnerability impact to developers
use_cases.description: Translate a finding into root cause, business impact, and practical mitigation guidance.

use_cases.title: Create secure coding training material
use_cases.description: Group common vulnerabilities by category for lessons, workshops, or onboarding.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a vulnerability
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain cross-site scripting using the vulnerability reference. Include definition, root cause, impact, and mitigation.

prompt_templates.title: Build a review checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a web application security review checklist from the reference for authentication, access control, API security, and data exposure.

prompt_templates.title: Map findings to OWASP
prompt_templates.prompt: Map these findings to OWASP Top 10 categories and summarize likely causes and mitigations: IDOR, weak TLS, missing security headers, and SSRF.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize remediation
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a SaaS application with APIs, file uploads, and admin workflows, prioritize the top vulnerability categories to review first and explain why.

output_examples.input: Explain IDOR for a product team.
output_examples.output: IDOR happens when a user can directly request another user's object without authorization checks. The likely impact is data exposure or unauthorized actions. Mitigation should focus on server-side access checks for every object request.

output_examples.input: Create a quick API security review outline.
output: Confirm every endpoint requires authentication where needed.
Check authorization decisions for each object and action.
Review input validation and rate limiting.
Verify API keys and tokens are stored and rotated securely.

output_examples.input: Summarize risks from missing security headers.
output_examples.output: Missing browser security headers can increase exposure to cross-site scripting, clickjacking, content sniffing, and downgrade attacks. Recommended controls include CSP, frame protection, content type controls, and HSTS.

best_practices: Use the reference to guide authorized security reviews only.
Pair each vulnerability category with application-specific evidence before reporting.
Adapt mitigations to the target stack, deployment model, and business workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the catalog as proof that a specific vulnerability exists.
Do not run tests against systems without permission.
Do not rely on this reference as a replacement for current advisories or threat intelligence.

faq.question: Does this skill scan applications?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference material for understanding and organizing web security risks.

faq.question: Is this only for OWASP Top 10 issues?
faq.answer: No. It includes OWASP mapping plus additional API, mobile, IoT, SSRF, and business logic categories.

faq.question: Can developers use it during remediation?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains root causes and mitigation themes that developers can adapt to their codebase.

faq.question: Does it include exploit code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is written as a reference guide and does not include executable exploit payloads.

faq.question: How current is the vulnerability list?
faq.answer: It is a static reference. Users should verify details against current OWASP, CWE, vendor, and threat intelligence sources.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Security engineers, developers, auditors, and team leads can use it for review planning and security education.
